data.title: Arcosa Refocuses On Infrastructure As Barge Sale Reshapes Earnings Story
data.description:
• Arcosa (NYSE:ACA) announced the sale of its barge business and is shifting its focus toward construction materials and engineered structures.
  • The move is aimed at concentrating resources on U.S. infrastructure and power related businesses that management views as core to the company.

Arcosa, trading at $111.28 at the last close, is coming off a strong longer term stretch, with the stock up 37.0% over the past year and 87.8% over three years. The company is also ahead 76.5% over five years, which gives context for this portfolio reshaping away from barges and toward its construction materials and engineered structures operations.

For investors, the barge sale is less about what Arcosa is exiting and more about where it plans to put its capital next. As the company increases its focus on infrastructure and power market exposure in the U.S., the key questions will be how efficiently it reinvests proceeds and whether the more focused portfolio affects its competitive position over time.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Arcosa by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Arcosa.

We've flagged 1 risk for Arcosa. See which could impact your investment.

Arcosa’s decision to sell its barge business for $450 million in cash comes on the back of a year where both revenue and profitability moved higher, with 2025 sales at $2,883.4 million and net income at $208.4 million. The barge exit reduces exposure to more cyclical transportation markets and allows management to lean further into construction materials and engineered structures, where the company already has meaningful scale. With 2026 revenue guidance of $2.95 billion to $3.10 billion and net income guidance of $224.4 million to $249.5 million, investors now have a clearer view of how a more focused Arcosa is expected to perform once the portfolio is reshaped.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Arcosa Narrative

  • The move to concentrate on construction materials and engineered structures lines up with the narrative that Arcosa is closely tied to U.S. infrastructure spending and grid modernization, including demand for utility structures for data centers and renewables.
  • The sale increases reliance on public infrastructure and power markets, which the narrative already flags as a risk if government spending or project timing changes, so the company becomes more exposed to those cycles.
  • The cash from the sale and any reinvestment choices are not fully captured in earlier narrative assumptions, particularly around future acquisitions or capacity additions that could change margins and earnings resilience.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Arcosa to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ A more concentrated focus on infrastructure and power projects could increase sensitivity to changes in U.S. government and utility spending levels or project delays.
  • ⚠️ Analysts have flagged Arcosa’s debt profile as a risk, so how the barge sale proceeds are used, whether for debt reduction or acquisitions, will matter for balance sheet strength.
  • 🎁 Earnings growth has recently been strong, with net income rising from $93.7 million in 2024 to $208.4 million in 2025, which supports the case for a more focused core business.
  • 🎁 The company has reported that its P/E of 26.2x sits below the Construction industry average of 33.6x, which some investors may view as supportive if they think Arcosa’s earnings profile is improving.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, it is worth watching how quickly Arcosa closes the barge sale and how management allocates the $450 million of cash, whether toward debt reduction, acquisitions or capacity in higher margin products like utility structures. The 2026 guidance will be an important reference point for how the more focused business tracks relative to expectations, especially if utility structure demand and wind tower activity move at different speeds. It also helps to compare Arcosa’s progress with peers in construction and engineered structures such as Martin Marietta, Vulcan Materials and Valmont, to see whether the sharpened portfolio helps or hurts its competitive standing over time.

data.title: Is Lennar (LEN) Pricing Fair After Recent Share Price Weakness?
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether Lennar's share price reflects its real worth, this article will walk through what the current market price might be implying about the company's value.
  • Lennar's stock closed at US$104.30, with returns of a 6.9% decline over 7 days, a 7.3% decline over 30 days, roughly flat at 0.1% year to date, and a 15.8% decline over 1 year, while the 3 year and 5 year returns sit at 19.6% and 33.5% respectively.
  • Recent share price moves have kept Lennar on the radar for investors looking at large US homebuilders, particularly as housing demand, financing conditions, and homebuilding activity remain in focus. These themes continue to shape how investors think about the sector's risks and opportunities, even outside of any single news headline.
  • Simply Wall St currently assigns Lennar a valuation score of 2 out of 6, which we will unpack using several valuation approaches, and then finish by looking at a broader way to think about value that goes beyond any single model.

Lennar scores just 2/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Lennar Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model projects a company’s future cash flows and then discounts them back to today’s dollars to estimate what the business could be worth right now.

For Lennar, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, based on cash flow projections in US$. The latest twelve month free cash flow is about $65.42 million. Analysts provide estimates for several years, and Simply Wall St then extrapolates these further, with the ten year projection reaching around $1.68b of free cash flow in 2035 before discounting.

Bringing all of those future cash flows back to today gives an estimated intrinsic value of $93.86 per share. Compared with the recent share price of $104.30, the DCF output suggests Lennar is around 11.1% overvalued on this model.

In short, the cash flow based math points to a stock that screens above its DCF fair value right now.

Result: OVERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Lennar may be overvalued by 11.1%. Discover 47 high quality undervalued stocks or create your own screener to find better value opportunities.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Lennar.

Approach 2: Lennar Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company like Lennar, the P/E ratio is a useful shorthand for how much investors are currently paying for each dollar of earnings. It ties the share price directly to the bottom line, which is usually the main driver of long term equity value for established businesses.

What counts as a normal or fair P/E often reflects how the market views a company’s growth prospects and risk profile. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher P/E, while slower expected growth or higher risk tends to support a lower multiple.

Lennar currently trades on a P/E of 12.55x. That is close to the Consumer Durables industry average of about 12.40x and the peer group average of 12.32x, so on simple comparisons the stock sits in a similar range to its sector. Simply Wall St also calculates a proprietary Fair Ratio for Lennar of 18.54x, which is the P/E it might trade on given factors such as earnings growth, profitability, industry, market cap and risk profile. This Fair Ratio can be more informative than a straight peer or industry comparison because it adjusts for company specific characteristics.

With the current P/E of 12.55x sitting below the Fair Ratio of 18.54x, this approach suggests the shares may be undervalued on an earnings multiple basis.

Result: UNDERVALUED

For Lennar, we’ll make it really easy for you with previews of two leading Lennar Narratives:

Each one connects a view on future earnings, margins and the housing backdrop to a Fair Value and a simple share price path. You can use them as reference points and then adjust the assumptions to match your own outlook.

🐂 Lennar Bull Case

Fair Value: US$162.49

Implied discount to Fair Value versus the recent US$104.30 share price: about 35.8% undervalued based on this narrative.

Revenue growth assumption: 5.67% per year

  • Sees Lennar and the wider homebuilding sector in a housing shortage over the long run, with US supply still several million units short of estimated demand, especially in high growth regions like the Sun Belt.
  • Builds a 1 year Fair Value path around earnings of about US$4.0b and a slightly higher P/E than today, then extends to a 3 year target using mid single digit revenue growth and profit margins around 10% to 11%.
  • Expects policy support, potential rate cuts and affordability initiatives to gradually unlock pent up demand after a period of weak transactions, with Lennar positioned in more affordable price points such as Texas communities well below the national median price.

🐻 Lennar Bear Case

  • Ties a US$95.00 bearish analyst price target to earnings of about US$2.0b by around 2028, a P/E of 13.3x on those earnings and an 8.5% discount rate, with additional attention on antitrust headlines and inventory clearance efforts.

data.title: Brilliant Earth Group (BRLT) EPS Loss Replaces Prior Profit And Tests Bullish Turnaround Narratives
data.description:
Brilliant Earth Group (BRLT) has wrapped up FY 2025 with fourth quarter revenue of US$124.4 million and a basic EPS loss of US$0.19, alongside trailing twelve month revenue of US$437.5 million and a basic EPS loss of US$0.25. This sets a clear earnings reset for investors to assess. Over recent periods, the company has seen quarterly revenue move from US$119.5 million in Q4 2024 to US$124.4 million in Q4 2025. Basic EPS shifted from US$0.03 in Q4 2024 to a loss of US$0.19 in Q4 2025, and trailing twelve month EPS moved from US$0.04 to a loss of US$0.25. With these results, the focus now turns to whether margins can improve enough to close the gap between ongoing losses and the revenue base.

  • Over the last twelve months, Brilliant Earth generated US$437.5 million in revenue but reported a net loss of US$3.6 million and a trailing EPS loss of US$0.25, compared with a trailing EPS of US$0.04 a year earlier.
  • What stands out for the bearish narrative is that losses have grown at about 81.3% per year over the past five years, and the latest quarterly EPS moved from a profit of US$0.03 in Q4 2024 to a loss of US$0.19 in Q4 2025. This lines up with concerns about pressure on margins and the risk that higher costs and competition could keep profitability under strain.
    • Bears point to slower forecast revenue growth of 5.1% per year versus 10.2% for the broader US market, and the trailing loss of US$3.6 million adds weight to the idea that lower growth may leave less room to absorb rising costs.
    • They also highlight the pattern of unprofitability over the last twelve months as a concrete data point behind worries that showroom expansion, compliance spending, and pricing pressure from lab grown diamonds could keep net income under pressure even with a solid revenue base.

  • Supporters of the bullish narrative focus on that gap, arguing that if profitability improves from this loss position, the business could look very different. Yet the combination of current unprofitability and below market revenue growth means that the low multiple and the roughly US$2.21 gap between the share price and DCF fair value are being weighed against the practical challenge of lifting margins from a trailing loss of US$3.6 million.

data.title: EyePoint Therapeutics (EYPT) Reports US$67.6 Million Quarterly Loss Testing Bullish Growth Narratives

    • Analysts in that balanced view also expect margins to move from deeply negative levels toward an industry average margin of 23.2%, while recent quarterly results show losses ranging from US$45.2 million to US$67.6 million, so the current cost base is still very high relative to sales.

    • Even using a single analyst target level of US$37.08 against the current US$15.76 share price, the gap is large compared with the underlying revenue base of US$31.4 million, which is why many investors focus on how realistic those growth and margin shifts look against the recent numbers.
